race.name: Adelaide Epic Ride
race.display_name: Adelaide Epic Ride (Santos Tour Down Under)
race.tagline: 136 km through the Adelaide Hills on the same roads as the UCI WorldTour pros. Chandlers Hill, Wickham Hill, Mount Barker Summit, and 1,800 metres of climbing under the January sun. The community ride of Australia's biggest professional cycling race.

vitals.date: Third or fourth Friday of January annually, during Santos Tour Down Under week
terrain_types: Sealed road through Adelaide suburbs and Adelaide Hills
Punchy categorised climbs including Chandlers Hill, Wickham Hill, and Mount Barker Summit
Fast descents and rolling terrain through South Australian wine country
vitals.start_time: Friday 5:45 AM from Henley Beach (136km); 7:00 AM from Flagstaff Hill (116km); 9:30 AM from Meadows (63km)
vitals.registration: Online via AusCycling / adelaideepic.org. 136km ~$225 AUD, 116km ~$200 AUD, 63km ~$149 AUD (AusCycling member pricing). Non-members pay ~$13 AUD surcharge for one-day license. Early bird and launch pricing available.
vitals.prize_purse: None. This is a non-competitive community ride.
vitals.aid_stations: Multiple on-course rest stops with water, nutrition, and mechanical support. Shuttle bus service available from finish to start locations.
vitals.cutoff_time: Generous time limit with on-course support. Riders typically finish between 4-8 hours depending on distance and ability.
distance_options: 136 km (Henley Beach to Nairne — full Ziptrak Stage 3 route, 1,800 m climbing)
116 km (Flagstaff Hill to Nairne — same route minus the coastal flat, 1,600 m climbing)
63 km (Meadows to Nairne — final third of the route with significant climbing)
vitals.entry_fee: ~$225 AUD (136km); ~$200 AUD (116km); ~$149 AUD (63km). Includes jersey, on-course support, and event village access. Shuttle bus booked separately.

climate.primary: Adelaide summer — hot, dry, 18-35C with possibility of extreme heat days exceeding 40C
climate.description: Late January in Adelaide is peak summer. Expect pre-dawn start temperatures of 16-20C rising rapidly to 30-38C by mid-morning. Adelaide regularly records days above 40C in January, and the Adelaide Hills provide minimal shade on exposed climb faces. Humidity is typically low, which helps with cooling but accelerates dehydration. The morning start at 5:45 AM is deliberately designed to get riders through the hardest climbing before the worst of the afternoon heat. Sea breezes from Gulf St Vincent can moderate coastal sections but rarely penetrate the Adelaide Hills. Riders finishing the 136km route in 6+ hours will face the full force of South Australian summer heat.
challenges: Extreme heat potential — Adelaide regularly exceeds 35C in late January, with 40C+ heatwave days possible
Dehydration risk across 136 km of climbing in summer conditions — carry extra water and electrolytes
Exposed climb faces with limited shade — Chandlers Hill and Mount Barker Summit bake in direct sun
Pre-dawn start temperature differential — cool at 5:45 AM, potentially 20C+ warmer by finish
Low humidity dries sweat quickly, masking fluid loss — drink before you feel thirsty

terrain.primary: Sealed road through Adelaide Hills with categorised climbs
terrain.surface: Sealed roads throughout, following the Ziptrak Men's Stage 3 route of the Santos Tour Down Under. The 136 km route begins at sea level on Henley Beach and heads inland through Adelaide's southern suburbs before entering the Adelaide Hills. The terrain transitions from flat coastal riding to rolling hills and punchy categorised climbs. Chandlers Hill, Wickham Hill, and Mount Barker Summit provide the signature vertical, with steep gradients and fast descents between. Road quality is excellent — this is a UCI WorldTour stage route, swept and maintained to professional racing standards for the event.
features: Rides the exact same route as the UCI WorldTour Men's Stage 3 on the same day
Chandlers Hill — punchy climb through the southern suburbs with steep gradients
Wickham Hill — sustained climb through the Adelaide Hills with sweeping views
Mount Barker Summit — the signature climb less than 5 km from the finish in Nairne
1,800 metres of total climbing across 136 km — significantly more vertical than the flat Adelaide stereotype suggests

gravel_god_rating.tier_note: The community ride component of Australia's premier professional cycling race, the opening event of the UCI WorldTour calendar. Riding a WorldTour stage route on the same day as the pros carries genuine prestige (4) and delivers an experience (4) that few community rides anywhere in the world can match. The Adelaide Hills climbing — 1,800 m over 136 km — elevates this beyond a flat city ride. T2 at 64, with the UCI WorldTour association, 20+ years of history, and South Australian cycling culture lifting it above the field of community rides. Prestige of 4 would promote one tier under p4 rules, but p4 cannot promote into T1, so remains T2.
gravel_god_rating.score_note: Logistics=4 (Adelaide Airport, good transport, professional TDU infrastructure). Length=3 (136 km is solid but not ultra-distance). Technicality=1 (sealed roads throughout). Elevation=3 (1,800 m climbing with punchy categorised climbs). Climate=3 (January heat is a genuine challenge — 35C+ days common). Altitude=1 (sea level to modest Adelaide Hills). Adventure=3 (WorldTour stage route, Adelaide Hills scenery). Prestige=4 (UCI WorldTour association, ride the same roads as the pros on the same day). Race_quality=4 (TDU infrastructure, professional organization). Experience=4 (unique WorldTour stage ride, watching pros race the same route later). Community=4 (5,000-7,000+ riders, TDU festival atmosphere). Field_depth=1 (non-competitive). Value=3 ($225 AUD for 136km with jersey and support). Expenses=3 (Adelaide is affordable by Australian standards but January = peak season). Sum=41 + cultural_impact=4 = 45. Score = round(45/70 * 100) = 64.

logistics.explanation: Adelaide Airport (ADL) handles direct flights from all Australian state capitals, plus connections through Sydney and Melbourne to international hubs. The airport is 8 km from the city centre — a 15-minute taxi ride. Adelaide's compact grid layout and efficient public transport make getting around straightforward. The 136 km start at Henley Beach is accessible by tram and car from the CBD. For the 116 km and 63 km distances, shuttle bus options are available from the finish at Nairne back to Flagstaff Hill and Meadows respectively. The event operates under the Santos Tour Down Under umbrella — Events South Australia and NX Sports have been running TDU-associated rides for over two decades. Road closures are coordinated with SA Police, rest stops are professionally stocked, and the entire infrastructure of a UCI WorldTour race stage supports the community ride.

length.explanation: The headline distance of 136 km is a solid day in the saddle but sits below the 160+ km threshold that separates genuinely long events from merely substantial ones. The climbing — 1,800 metres — adds considerable physical demand that the raw distance does not capture. Three distance options (136 km, 116 km, 63 km) ensure accessibility, but the longest option is the one that carries the cultural weight. For experienced road cyclists, 136 km with 1,800 m of climbing is a challenging but not extreme day. For the average community rider, it is a genuine test. The early 5:45 AM start reflects the organisers' understanding that six-plus hours of riding in Adelaide summer heat requires getting kilometres done before the worst of the afternoon.

technicality.explanation: Sealed roads throughout, following a UCI WorldTour stage route. The descents off the Adelaide Hills climbs are fast but on wide, well-maintained roads swept to professional racing standards. No gravel, no singletrack, no technical terrain of any kind. The challenge is cardiovascular and thermal, not technical. If you can ride a road bike on sealed roads, you have the technical skills for this event.

elevation.explanation: 1,800 metres of climbing over 136 km is respectable by any measure and puts this firmly above the flat sportive category. The climbing comes in distinct punches rather than one sustained effort — Chandlers Hill, Wickham Hill, and Mount Barker Summit are all categorised climbs with steep gradients that demand honest climbing legs. The route rises from sea level at Henley Beach into the Adelaide Hills, gaining most of its vertical in the second half. Mount Barker Summit, arriving less than 5 km from the finish in Nairne, is the final test when legs are at their most depleted. This is not an Alpine epic, but it is not flat either. The Adelaide Hills have a way of surprising riders who expect pancake-flat South Australia.

climate.explanation: Late January in Adelaide means summer heat, full stop. Average maximums sit around 29-32C, but heatwave days exceeding 40C are not uncommon — Adelaide recorded 46.6C in January 2019. Even on a typical day, riders finishing the 136 km route after midday will face 30-35C temperatures on exposed climb faces with limited shade. The dry heat is deceptive — low humidity means sweat evaporates quickly, masking the rate of fluid loss. The organisers mitigate this with the pre-dawn 5:45 AM start, but physics and geography are unforgiving. Carry more water than you think you need. Electrolyte supplementation is not optional. The climate score of 3 reflects that while Adelaide heat is a genuine challenge, it is a predictable one — you know what you are riding into, and you can prepare for it.

altitude.explanation: The route climbs from sea level to approximately 500 metres in the Adelaide Hills. Altitude is irrelevant to performance at these elevations. Your lungs will work identically at Henley Beach and atop Mount Barker Summit. The challenge in the hills is gradient and heat, not thin air.

adventure.explanation: The adventure here is experiential rather than geographical. You are riding the same roads that WorldTour professionals will race on later that same day. You will climb the same hills, descend the same roads, and finish in the same town. The Adelaide Hills — rolling vineyards, eucalyptus-lined roads, small towns like Hahndorf and Nairne — deliver genuine scenic appeal that surprises riders expecting flat farmland. The Ziptrak Stage 3 route from the coast through the hills to the finish is a well-designed journey with narrative arc: flat coastal start, transitional suburbs, then the hills reveal themselves. It is not wilderness riding, but it is a meaningful journey through distinctive South Australian landscape.

prestige.explanation: This is the community ride component of the Santos Tour Down Under — the opening event of the UCI WorldTour calendar and the biggest professional cycling race in the Southern Hemisphere. Riding the actual WorldTour stage route on the same day as the pros is a privilege that very few community rides worldwide can offer. The event has operated continuously for over 20 years under various names — the Bupa Challenge Tour (2003-2017), and now the Adelaide Epic Ride (from 2025). André Greipel serves as a 2026 ambassador. Eddy Merckx has ridden the community ride. The prestige comes through association with the professional race rather than from the community ride itself, but that association is genuine and deeply embedded.

race_quality.explanation: The Adelaide Epic Ride operates within the Santos Tour Down Under infrastructure ecosystem. Road closures are coordinated by SA Police for the WorldTour race — the community ride benefits from the same traffic management. Rest stops are professionally stocked with water, nutrition, and mechanical support. NX Sports, the third-party organiser, has extensive experience with mass-participation cycling events. AusCycling provides the insurance and regulatory framework. Shuttle buses run from the finish at Nairne back to start locations. A 12-week training guide is provided to registered riders. The quality of organisation reflects the standards demanded by a UCI WorldTour event — this is not a local club ride with a registration form.

experience.explanation: The pre-dawn start at Henley Beach as the summer sun comes up over the Adelaide skyline. Five thousand riders rolling through the quiet streets before the city wakes up. The flat coastal kilometres building your legs and your confidence before the terrain tilts upward. Chandlers Hill — the first real climb, a shock to legs that have been spinning on flat road. The Adelaide Hills opening up around you: vineyards, gum trees, small towns, the smell of eucalyptus in the morning heat. Wickham Hill testing your climbing legs mid-ride. Then Mount Barker Summit — the final climb with less than 5 km to go, when everything hurts and the finish at Nairne is almost within reach. And then the knowledge that in a few hours, the WorldTour peloton will race these same roads at twice your speed. That context — riding a professional stage route on race day — elevates the experience beyond a standard sportive.

community.explanation: Five thousand to seven thousand riders across all distances, ranging from weekend warriors to club racers to tourists who have travelled specifically for the Tour Down Under festival. The event is embedded in Adelaide's January cycling culture — the city transforms into a cycling carnival for 10 days, with group rides, the Tour Village in Victoria Square, and the professional racing creating a community atmosphere that pervades the city. The community ride has run in various forms for over 20 years, building generational participation. Cancer Council SA partnerships have added a charitable dimension. Adelaide's cycling community is tight-knit and welcoming — visiting riders are absorbed into the festival atmosphere immediately.

field_depth.explanation: Zero competitive depth. This is a community ride with no timing, no results, no age group categories, no podium, and no prize money. Fast riders will push hard on the climbs, and informal group efforts will form naturally, but officially this is a participation event. If you want to race on these roads, qualify for the WorldTour.

value.explanation: The 136 km entry at $225 AUD (~$150 USD) includes a custom cycling jersey, on-course rest stops with nutrition and mechanical support, road closures and traffic management, and access to the finish-line event village. The jersey is a tangible inclusion that offsets some of the entry cost. Shuttle buses from finish to start locations are available but booked separately at additional cost. For the scale of the event and the quality of organisation, the pricing is fair but not cheap — comparable Australian sportives offer similar inclusions at lower price points. The shorter distance options at $200 and $149 AUD offer better per-kilometre value. Early bird pricing (from $172.80 AUD for 136km) significantly improves the equation.

expenses.explanation: Adelaide is one of the more affordable Australian capital cities. Hotels in the CBD run $120-250 AUD per night during Tour Down Under week — peak season pricing but not Melbourne or Sydney levels. Airbnb and serviced apartment options in the Adelaide Hills add character but may require a car. Domestic flights from Melbourne or Sydney are $100-300 AUD return. Adelaide's compact city centre keeps transport and food costs reasonable — you can walk to most things. For international visitors, Adelaide is a secondary destination that typically requires a connection through Sydney or Melbourne, adding flight time and cost. Budget $1,200-2,500 AUD for flights, accommodation, entry, and expenses over a long weekend. Wrap it into a broader Tour Down Under spectating trip to maximise value.

biased_opinion.verdict: The Pro Stage You Can Ride
biased_opinion.summary: The Adelaide Epic Ride is the rare community ride that lets you put your wheels on a genuine UCI WorldTour stage route and ride it on the same day as the professionals. The 136 km route from Henley Beach to Nairne through the Adelaide Hills delivers 1,800 metres of honest climbing — Chandlers Hill, Wickham Hill, and Mount Barker Summit are real climbs, not token hills — under the full force of South Australian summer heat. This is not the hardest sportive in Australia. But it is the one most directly connected to the highest level of professional cycling, and that connection is what makes it special.
strengths: WorldTour stage route: you ride the exact same roads the professionals will race on later that day — a privilege almost no other community ride offers
Real climbing: 1,800 m of elevation gain with punchy categorised climbs that demand genuine fitness, not a flat parade
Tour Down Under festival: the ride is embedded in a 10-day cycling carnival that transforms Adelaide into a cycling city
Organisation quality: UCI WorldTour infrastructure backing a community ride means road closures, support, and logistics are professional-grade
History: over 20 years of continuous operation under various names — the Bupa Challenge Tour legacy lives on
weaknesses: Heat: late January in Adelaide can be brutal — 35C+ days are common and 40C+ heatwaves are possible. This is not a risk you can ignore.
Non-competitive: no timing, no results, no racing. If you need a number and a clock, look elsewhere.
Price: $225 AUD for 136 km is at the upper end of Australian community ride pricing. The jersey helps but does not fully justify it.
Route changes annually: because the ride follows the WorldTour stage, the route changes each year when stages are redesigned. You cannot train on the exact course until it is announced.
biased_opinion.bottom_line: Five thousand riders on a WorldTour stage route in the Adelaide Hills, in January heat, with the pros racing the same roads hours later. That is the proposition. If that does not appeal to you, nothing about this event will. If it does, book early — it sells out.

final_verdict.one_liner: 136 km on a UCI WorldTour stage route through the Adelaide Hills with 1,800 m of climbing, ridden on the same day as the pros. The community ride of Australia's biggest professional cycling race.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want the unique experience of riding a genuine WorldTour stage route on race day, embedded in the Santos Tour Down Under festival atmosphere. Yes if you can handle South Australian summer heat and want a well-supported 136 km ride with real climbing. Skip if you need competitive racing, if you cannot tolerate extreme heat, or if you find $225 AUD steep for a community ride.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want an Australian community ride with similar WorldTour connections: try Cadel Evans Great Ocean Road Race - People's Ride (Victoria, March). If you want more distance and community in Australia: try Around the Bay in a Day (Melbourne, October, 210 km). If you want a mass-participation ride with serious climbing in summer heat: try L'Etape Australia.

logistics.airport: Adelaide Airport (ADL) — direct flights from all Australian state capitals (Sydney, Melbourne, Brisbane, Perth, Darwin, Hobart, Canberra). International connections primarily through Sydney and Melbourne. Airport is 8 km from the CBD, 15 minutes by taxi. Henley Beach start (136 km) is approximately 5 km from the airport.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Adelaide CBD offers the best base — compact, walkable, and within easy reach of Henley Beach by car or tram. Hotels range from $120-250 AUD per night during TDU week. Glenelg and Henley Beach offer beachside accommodation close to the 136 km start. The Adelaide Hills (Hahndorf, Stirling, Mount Lofty) provide character stays but require a car. Book early — Tour Down Under week is Adelaide's peak tourism period and quality accommodation fills fast. Consider staying the full TDU week to watch professional racing stages and attend festival events.
logistics.food: Adelaide's food and wine culture punches well above its weight. The Central Market is a pre-ride carb-loading destination. Rundle Street and Gouger Street offer everything from Asian to Italian to modern Australian. The Adelaide Hills are wine country — Hahndorf's German heritage restaurants and the Barossa Valley are within easy reach for rest days. Post-ride, Nairne's options are limited — most riders shuttle back to Adelaide for celebration meals. The Tour Village in Victoria Square is the social hub during TDU week.
logistics.packet_pickup: Event information and jersey collection details published on adelaideepic.org. Check for pre-event pickup locations and times. Jerseys, timing tags, and rider information packs collected prior to event day. AusCycling membership verification required.
logistics.parking: Henley Beach (136 km start) has limited street parking — arrive early or arrange drop-off. Flagstaff Hill (116 km start) and Meadows (63 km start) have designated parking areas. Shuttle bus services run from Nairne finish back to start locations — book separately through the event website. Public transport options available from Adelaide CBD to Henley Beach via tram.

history.founder: Events South Australia (as part of Santos Tour Down Under)
history.origin_story: The community ride component of the Santos Tour Down Under began in 2003 as the Bupa Challenge Tour, sponsored by private healthcare provider Bupa. The concept was simple and compelling: give amateur cyclists the chance to ride the same stage route as the UCI WorldTour professionals, on the same day, just hours before the peloton rolled through. Bupa's sponsorship ran until 2017, during which time the ride became a fixture of the Tour Down Under festival, regularly attracting 5,000-7,000+ riders across multiple distances. The ride covered various stage routes over the years — through the Barossa Valley to Tanunda, from Norwood through the Adelaide Hills, and across South Australia's diverse cycling terrain. Following the cancellation of TDU during 2020-2022 due to COVID-19 and subsequent restructuring, the community ride was relaunched in 2025 as the Adelaide Epic Ride for the 25th anniversary edition of the Tour Down Under. NX Sports now manages the event as a third-party associated event. The rebrand brought a fresh identity while preserving the core proposition: ride a WorldTour stage, earn your bragging rights, and be part of Australia's biggest professional cycling event.
notable_moments: 2003: Inaugural community ride as part of the Santos Tour Down Under, sponsored by Bupa as the 'Bupa Challenge Tour'
2012: 10th edition of the Bupa Challenge Tour attracts 7,036 registered riders — 5,000 on the full 138 km distance from Norwood to Tanunda in the Barossa Valley
2013: Route from Modbury to Tanunda with four distance options (127 km, 92 km, 46.5 km, 20 km). Cancer Council SA partnership established for 'Ride for a reason' fundraising.
2014: Bupa renews Tour Down Under sponsorship for three additional years, extending Bupa Challenge Tour naming rights through 2017
Eddy Merckx and other cycling legends have participated in the community ride, adding to its prestige and cultural significance
2020-2022: Tour Down Under cancelled or significantly modified due to COVID-19 — community ride suspended
2025: Relaunched as the Adelaide Epic Ride for the 25th anniversary edition of TDU. Three distances: 120 km, 85 km, 20 km from Norwood to Uraidla.
2026: Route follows Ziptrak Stage 3 from Henley Beach to Nairne — 136 km, 116 km, and 63 km options. André Greipel serves as event ambassador.
history.reputation: The community ride of Australia's biggest professional cycling race. Over 20 years of operation under two names — the Bupa Challenge Tour and the Adelaide Epic Ride — have embedded this event in South Australian cycling culture. The proposition is unique and enduring: ride a WorldTour stage route on race day, in the January heat, and earn your place in the Tour Down Under story.

course_description.character: A sealed-road journey from the Adelaide coast through the southern suburbs and into the Adelaide Hills, following the exact route of the UCI WorldTour Men's Stage 3. The 136 km route begins at Henley Beach at sea level and heads south and east through Adelaide's suburban fringe before the terrain tilts decisively upward into the Adelaide Hills. The character shifts dramatically across the ride: flat coastal kilometres give way to rolling suburban hills, which in turn yield to the punchy categorised climbs that define the stage. Chandlers Hill arrives first — a short, sharp shock to legs that have been spinning on flat road. Wickham Hill follows, a more sustained effort through Adelaide Hills bushland. The final act is Mount Barker Summit, arriving with less than 5 km to the finish in Nairne, when accumulated fatigue and January heat conspire to make every gradient feel twice as steep. The descents between climbs are fast and rewarding — wide, well-surfaced roads swept to professional racing standards.

suffering_zones.label: The Coastal Flat
suffering_zones.desc: Rolling out of Henley Beach at 5:45 AM in pre-dawn summer light, five thousand riders filling the road as the Adelaide skyline wakes up to the east. The first 30 km are flat and fast along the coast and through Adelaide's southern suburbs — a chance to settle into rhythm, manage your effort, and absorb the scale of the event. The ocean breeze at your back is a gift you will remember fondly when the hills arrive. Use this section to eat, drink, and find a group. The climbing ahead will be easier in company.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Flat sealed roads, coastal then suburban, sea level to 50 m elevation

suffering_zones.label: Chandlers Hill
suffering_zones.desc: The first real test. Chandlers Hill rises through Adelaide's southern suburbs — a categorised climb with gradients that remind you this is a WorldTour stage, not a coastal cruise. The climb is short but punchy, and it arrives just as the terrain begins its decisive shift from flat suburban roads to rolling Adelaide Hills landscape. Pace yourself here. The temptation to attack the first climb is strong, but you have 100 km and two bigger climbs still ahead. Chandlers Hill is a gateway, not a destination.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Sealed road, categorised climb, steep gradients, suburban to semi-rural transition

suffering_zones.label: The Adelaide Hills Rollers
suffering_zones.desc: Between the categorised climbs, the Adelaide Hills deliver relentless rolling terrain — short rises and fast descents through eucalyptus bushland, vineyards, and small towns. The accumulated vertical is deceptive. No single roller is hard, but the constant up-and-down drains your legs and your patience. The scenery compensates — the Adelaide Hills are genuinely beautiful, with long views across vineyards and bushland that make you forget you are 40 km from a capital city. The heat is building now. The morning cool is a memory. Drink consistently.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Rolling sealed roads, 200-400 m elevation, eucalyptus and vineyard landscape

suffering_zones.label: Wickham Hill
suffering_zones.desc: The second categorised climb and the longest sustained effort on the route. Wickham Hill demands honest climbing legs — the gradient is consistent rather than steep, but the length of the effort, combined with accumulated fatigue from the preceding 90+ km, makes this the ride's attritional centrepiece. By the time you reach the top, you have climbed over 1,200 metres and the January sun is directly overhead. The descent off the back is fast and welcome — tuck in, recover, and prepare mentally for the final test.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Sealed road, sustained categorised climb, Adelaide Hills bushland

suffering_zones.label: Mount Barker Summit
suffering_zones.desc: The final climb, arriving with less than 5 km to the finish in Nairne. Mount Barker Summit is where the Adelaide Epic earns its name. Your legs carry 120+ km of fatigue. The heat is at its peak. The gradient does not care about your excuses. This climb breaks the riders who have overcooked the earlier sections — pacing across the entire ride is vindicated or punished on Mount Barker Summit. The finish at Nairne is close enough to smell but far enough to hurt. Grind over the top, descend into Nairne, and cross the line knowing you have ridden the same roads the WorldTour pros will race in a few hours.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Sealed road, categorised climb, final kilometres to Nairne finish, ~450-500 m elevation

course_description.signature_challenge: Mount Barker Summit with less than 5 km to the finish — the final categorised climb after 130+ km of riding and 1,500+ metres of accumulated climbing in January heat. Every pedal stroke on this climb is earned. The gradient is honest, the heat is real, and the finish at Nairne is close enough to be cruel.

guide_variables.race_distance: 136 km (full); 116 km and 63 km options available
guide_variables.race_date: January (during Santos Tour Down Under week)
guide_variables.race_location: Henley Beach to Nairne, Adelaide, South Australia
guide_variables.race_terrain: Sealed road through Adelaide suburbs and Adelaide Hills with categorised climbs
guide_variables.race_weather: Adelaide summer — hot, dry, 18-35C+ with extreme heat potential. Pre-dawn start mitigates worst heat.
race_challenges: 1,800 metres of climbing with punchy categorised climbs — Chandlers Hill, Wickham Hill, Mount Barker Summit
January heat in Adelaide — 30-38C typical, 40C+ heatwave days possible
Mount Barker Summit after 130+ km of riding — the sting in the tail when legs are most depleted
Hydration and nutrition management across 5-7 hours in summer heat conditions
Pacing discipline — the flat coastal start encourages going too hard before the hills arrive

non_negotiables.requirement: Heat adaptation — minimum two weeks of training in hot conditions before the event
non_negotiables.why: Adelaide in late January can exceed 35C, with 40C+ heatwave days not uncommon. Your body needs physiological adaptation to perform in extreme heat — increased plasma volume, earlier sweat onset, and improved cardiovascular stability. Train in the hottest part of the day for at least two weeks before the event. If you live in a cold climate, use indoor training with added heat (sauna, heated room, extra layers) to simulate conditions. Arriving in Adelaide without heat preparation and attempting 136 km of climbing in full summer sun is a recipe for heat exhaustion.

non_negotiables.requirement: One training ride of 100+ km with 1,200+ metres of climbing in warm conditions
non_negotiables.why: The Adelaide Epic demands sustained climbing on fatigued legs in heat. A flat 100 km ride does not prepare you for this. You need a training ride that combines genuine distance with genuine climbing — 100+ km with at least 1,200 m of vertical — ridden in the warmest conditions you can find. This ride will reveal your nutrition strategy gaps, your hydration weaknesses, and your pacing discipline. Do not arrive in Adelaide having never ridden more than 80 km in training.

non_negotiables.requirement: Hydration strategy tested and proven — 500-750 ml per hour plus electrolytes
non_negotiables.why: In 30-38C dry heat, your body can lose 1-2 litres of sweat per hour. The rest stops provide water, but relying entirely on aid stations across 136 km of climbing is gambling with your health. Carry two large bottles minimum. Know your sweat rate. Test your electrolyte mix (sodium, potassium, magnesium) on long training rides in heat. Cramping on Mount Barker Summit at km 130 because you under-hydrated in the first half is preventable with preparation.

videos.title: Thousands take part in Amy's ride
videos.description: Thousands of cyclists have taken part in a charity ride from Adelaide to McLaren Vale in memory of cyclist Amy Gillett.

of the Southern suburbs in a show of
strength to promote Road Safety they
were taking part in a charity event in
memory of cyclist Amy
gillet from the start at Flinders

their way down the Southern Expressway

South Australian cyclist Amy gillet who

videos.title: Elen's First Gran Fondo!
videos.description:
🚴 Watch the Cycle Adventures TV series for FREE on CTV+
https://ctvplus.org.au/series/cycle-with-serafina/

We’re Serafina & Gus.

We ride. We film. We stop for coffee. We talk to strangers. We share the people, places and everyday magic you find when you travel slowly by bike.

This YouTube channel is where the heart of it all lives — the rides, the stories, and the journey behind the show. This was such a fun ride! Check it out on Strava, a mixture of bike paths and Adelaide Hills with a nice amount of climbing https://www.strava.com/activities/5250393160

Adelaide to Glenelg then along the coast to Marino, from there we jumped on the  Coast to Vines Trail to McLaren Vale where we stopped for lunch.

From McLaren Vale we took the Shiraz Trail to Willunga, up Willunga Hill then through the hills to Clarendon and beyond! 

Subscribe & follow on socials 👇

YouTube www.youtube.com/c/CyclewithSerafina

Strava https://www.strava.com/athletes/13876110

Insta https://www.instagram.com/cycle_with_serafina

FB https://www.facebook.com/cycleadelaidesa

See you on the road!
Serafina
xx

videos.curation_reason: First-person ride-along through Adelaide Hills with specific course details including Willunga Hill from the Santos Tour Down Under route.

videos.title: Cycling Lincoln Gap Loop in Middlebury, VT -  Great Rides Powered by Hyland's
videos.description:
No matter where you are, there's an epic ride just around the corner. Up next on our #GreatRides series: the Lincoln Gap Loop near Middlebury, VT! 

Download this ride from Strava: http://bit.ly/vtgreatride

This picturesque figure-eight ride, with covered bridges and rustic barns, epitomizes the very best of New England cycling. But don't be fooled by the charming scenery. With an average grade of 14.5% and gear-grinding max grade of 24%, Lincoln Gap is considered the steepest paved mile in the entire U.S...for a hill climb you won't soon forget. (Tip: Jump ahead to 2 min on the full video to check it out!)

This #cycling route was submitted by our friend Todd Warnock @gravel2gravel of the @VermontGranFondo. If you have a favorite ride that you’d like us to consider for a future video, please email its Strava link and a brief description of what makes it a Great Ride to hylandspowered@hylands.com.

Be sure to subscribe to this channel so that you’ll never miss an episode of Great Rides powered by Hyland’s.
videos.transcript:
today's ride combines the postcard
beauty of new england with one of the
nation's steepest paved climbs
featured in the vermont gran fondo this
multi-surface route provides a stiff
challenge for riders of all abilities
welcome to great rides powered by
highlands this is the lincoln gap loop
if you're heading to vermont this
must-do ride contains everything you'll
need to experience the wonder of this
region with both the middlebury gap and
the legendary lincoln gap
this figure 8 ride is just over 80 miles
long includes short stretches of gravel
and has a total elevation gain of 7 84
feet
don't be lulled by its scenic forest
flowing rivers and covered bridges
this ride has a real sting in its tail
and its legendary climbs are sought
after by cyclists from around the world
the bristol cliffs cafe in downtown
bristol is a popular meetup point with
plenty of parking you can grab some food
and fill your bottles before starting
your day
after a relaxing warm-up rolling out of
town you'll turn onto lincoln road where
the real fun begins
with a slight incline you'll wind your
way along the tree line new haven river
at mile 6 cross the river and turn south
for the first time as you continue to
climb
get ready you'll soon experience local
gravel as you roll
through vermont hills and then descend
into ripton don't miss the historic
ripton general store
it's a popular pit stop before your
first climb
east towards their first obstacle the
middlebury gap its average grade of just
3.8 percent belies in
ever-increasing steepness that ramps up
to 13 near the top
after cresting the climb you'll be
rewarded with a 9.5 mile descent that
eventually
leads to your next gravel section on
matson road
the route continues to serve up mixed
surfaces short climbs and descents until
you reach the halfway point in the town
of warren
a convenient place to stop and refuel is
the war and general store before you
face the grueling lincoln gap
once rested be sure to check out the war
and cover bridge as you head out of town
after a three mile approach you'll find
yourself at the base of lincoln gap
considered to be the steepest paved mile
in the u.s the lincoln gap is a hill you
won't soon forget
the climb is just over one mile long and
serves up an elevation gain of 1496 feet
its average grade is 14.5 percent but
the toughest section rears up to a gear
grinding
24 percent many riders are forced to
paper boy their way to the top
it's remote narrow and winding so even
though you'll be deep in the pain
cage stay alert for oncoming traffic and
other cyclists
it can get crowded with visitors at this
point but don't let that stop you from
reaching the top
the descent down the other side of
lincoln gap is steep and can be tricky
so take it easy
after about two miles of downhill you'll
find yourself back at the familiar
intersection of south lincoln road where
you'll turn
south once again for more river views
back in ripton this time head west
towards middlebury
appreciate your surroundings as you make
your way through tree-lined roads and
pastoral vermont farmland
turn right in new haven with only four
miles left and enjoy the classic vermont
barns that dot the road
take it all in and reflect on what
you've accomplished soon you'll be back
in bristol celebrating at the bobcat
cafe and brewery
if you're in the vermont area we hope
you're able to test yourself on the
lincoln gap loop
for more details and a link to the route
check the description below
and thanks to todd warnock of the
vermont gran fondo for sharing this ride
if you have a route that you'd like
featured on great rides powered by
highlands then email your strava link
and a description of why it's such a
great ride to hylandspower at
highlands.com
hyland's leg cramps stop your cramp not
your ride claims based on traditional
homeopathic practice not accepted
medical evidence
you

videos.title: Gran Fondo Ephrata 2022 Rollout and Finish
videos.description:
Gran Fondo Ephrata is the first gravel event of this very popular series. Viscious Cycle once again put on a great event on March 20, 2022. Ephrata Wa is usually windy and this year was no different, but the sun was out, the temps were quite mild and the wind was manageable. We had a great ride and look forward the the next one. You should go sign up for one if you've never done one.
http://rideviciouscycle.com/
Do it, do it.

videos.title: 1,080km Melbourne to Adelaide epic bike ride - mashup
videos.description:
Check out some of the awesome footage from my epic ride from Melbourne to Adelaide.

Check it out on Strava: https://www.strava.com/activities/493007639

quotes.text: We're going to go from here to McLaren Vale have lunch, and then after that we'll go on a bike path to Willunga and then we'll go Willunga hill - it's not in the Tour Down Under for nothing.

This is where the real climbing starts so we're fed up and got less energy to get up Willunga Hill which is really cool because they use this in the Tour Down Under all the time - we're going to be like Tour Down Under riders.

key_challenges.name: Willunga Hill
key_challenges.description: They use this in the tour down under all the time. Described as where 'the real climbing starts' after riders have already done 400 meters of elevation.

race_day_tips.text: Allow around seven hours for the full route. Stop at McLaren Vale for lunch - the Cottage Bakery is right next to the bike path and a popular refueling spot.

additional_quotes.text: This is our first course we've done and this is our hometown so this is uh we enjoy sharing this ride with everybody so it's great to have you all out here quick reminder this is a ride it's not a race so paint yourself out there have a good time with it

I was just like riding along and then hit it rolled over a stick or something can then snap and I broke a spoke and look at i just wanted to show you like look how much of a difference just from one spoke

rider_intel.search_text: The Adelaide Epic Ride covers approximately 100 kilometers with 1300 meters of elevation gain according to riders. The route goes from Glenelg to McLaren Vale, then continues on bike paths to Willunga before tackling Willunga Hill, which is featured in the Tour Down Under professional race. Riders describe Willunga Hill as where the real climbing starts, coming after already completing 400 meters of elevation. The Cottage Bakery in McLaren Vale is highlighted as a popular lunch stop located right next to the bike path. Riders should allow around seven hours for the full route. The event has a community atmosphere with local organizers emphasizing it's a ride rather than a race, encouraging participants to pace themselves and enjoy the experience. Mechanical issues like broken spokes can occur on the mixed terrain, so riders should be prepared for potential equipment problems during the ride.
